next_steps: Obtain Douce 128 f. 258 and inspect original lineation.
Compare DIMEV segmentation and later editions before amending either count.
openQuestions.question: Why do Morris, Heuser, and CME00096 display twenty short lines for the Douce 128 prayer while DIMEV describes sixteen?

next_steps: Request target images or a current foliation concordance from the British Library.
Do not infer an offset from the English poem's historical/current locator alone.
openQuestions.question: How do Wright and Heuser's historical f. 59 locator for the Latin Cotton Cleopatra B II poem and the current catalog component beginning at f. 60 reconcile?

next_steps: Use the recorded institution-specific request routes and ask first whether a complete preservation surrogate already exists.
Request complete codices where feasible and identify the target folios separately.
Preserve written reuse terms before placing any supplied image in Git or print-on-demand outputs.
openQuestions.question: Can whole-object facsimiles be obtained for Rawlinson poet. 175, Harley 4196, Cotton Cleopatra B II, Douce 126, Douce 128, Lincoln MS 91, CUL Dd.5.64, and Caius 175/96?

next_steps: Transcribe and search the full text against current Latin repertories.
Treat FitzRalph and Kilmington as subjects or contextual names until attribution evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: Can the Latin defense of friars in Trinity O.9.38 be attributed more securely than Heuser's discussion of Richard FitzRalph and Richard Kilmington permits?

next_steps: Keep all source-cache files Git-ignored and publish URLs, locators, and checksums only by default.
Treat Bodleian and Trinity CC BY-NC images as noncommercial; obtain separate commercial permission before inclusion in for-profit products.
Obtain written terms from BL, Lincoln, Caius, and CUL before redistributing newly supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Which cached or future manuscript images may be redistributed through a repository that also supports commercial print-on-demand use?

reviewNotes: The source graph deliberately separates direct manuscript transcription, excerpting, dependence on earlier editions, citation, and modern catalog description.
The lost Add MS 45896 version is collateral evidence discovered and published after Heuser; it is not a retroactive source of the 1904 article.
A complete published digital object can still be only a selected image set: the Douce bundles are complete representations of their one-canvas public objects, not of the codices.
All cached files are Git-ignored; each is hardlinked into both the CME00096 research worktree and the main checkout source-cache with matching inode and checksum.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00096 XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of Wilhelm Heuser's complete article 'With an O and an I,' Anglia 27, new series 15 (1904), pp. 283-319. The article is a heterogeneous edited selection with separate transmission paths: direct transcriptions from Bodleian Rawlinson poet. 175, British Library Cotton Cleopatra B II, Gonville and Caius MS 175/96, and Trinity College Cambridge O.9.38; selected Northern Homily excerpts from BL Harley 4196; passages re-edited from Thomas Wright and Richard Morris; and a Thornton/Cambridge composite chiefly after Carl Horstmann, with George Perry cited. A complete public 99-canvas IIIF facsimile of Trinity O.9.38 is cached and maps the target to canvases 62-63, ff. 54v-56r. Public Bodleian objects for Douce 126 and 128 contain only one non-overlapping opening each, Lincoln's catalog exposes one non-overlapping leaf, and no complete public facsimile was verified for the other direct witnesses. Complete public scans of every identified immediate printed source and the exact DIMEV 1.0 release are cached.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous medieval contributors
catalogSummary.date: Medieval texts chiefly attested in fourteenth- and fifteenth-century witnesses; edited selection published 1904
catalogSummary.region: England; precise composition places vary or remain unresolved
catalogSummary.title: With an O and an I

catalogingSubject.label: With an O and an I
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Wilhelm Heuser's complete 1904 Anglia article and edited selection: German commentary framing nine groups of Middle English and Latin verse transcribed or re-edited from several manuscripts and earlier editions. It is not a single medieval work, any one manuscript, the complete Anglia volume, or the U-M encoding.

contentParts.label: Heuser's editorial commentary

locators.value: 27 P elements plus four comparison L elements in article section 3

contentParts.summary: German introductions, source discussion, comparisons, notes, and addendum framing, with four Middle English refrain quotations in section 3.

contentParts.label: Four Evangelists refrain cycle
contentParts.summary: A complete unified cycle of 26 six-line stanzas: eight each for Luke and Matthew and five each for Mark and John.

contentParts.label: Expanded Northern Homily Cycle excerpts

locators.value: Luke f. 6r-v; Matthew ff. 28r-30r; John ff. 16v-17r

contentParts.summary: Eleven selected verse groups used to demonstrate Heuser's proposed source relationship; these are omissions-bearing extracts rather than complete homilies.

contentParts.label: Middle English poem against the Minorites
contentParts.summary: Seven six-line aabbcc stanzas directly transcribed by Heuser from Cotton Cleopatra B II.

contentParts.label: Latin poem against friars, stanzas 1 and 32

locators.value: 1 and 32 of On the Council of London

contentParts.summary: Two six-line stanzas selected from the 49-stanza Latin poem printed by Thomas Wright from Cotton Cleopatra B II.

contentParts.label: Godys sone, þat was so fre
contentParts.summary: A Passion song reorganized by Heuser from Morris's Douce 126 edition into five inferred twelve-line stanzas.

contentParts.label: As thou for holy church dyd pray
contentParts.summary: An acephalous prayer for which Heuser follows Morris and infers a two-stanza form; line-count reconciliation remains open.

contentParts.label: When Adam delved and Eve span
contentParts.summary: Heuser's eight-stanza short-line presentation of the Thornton version, with substantive Cambridge Dd.5.64 variants, chiefly after Horstmann.

contentParts.label: Hours of the Cross
contentParts.summary: A complete refrain poem directly transcribed from Caius MS 175/96; 96 Middle English poem lines plus five Latin hour/display headings encoded as L.

contentParts.label: Latin defense of the friars
contentParts.summary: Thirty six-line Latin stanzas directly transcribed from Trinity College Cambridge MS O.9.38.

assertion.notes: A discovery range summarizing heterogeneous medieval material, not a claim of common composition.

dateStatements.display: Chiefly later Middle Ages; dates differ by component and composition commonly predates the surviving copy
Second half of the fourteenth century in Bodleian MS Rawlinson poet. 175
notes: Heuser argues that the cycle adapts the Expanded Northern Homily Cycle and cannot be earlier than the mid-fourteenth century; the manuscript date is an attestation date, not an exact composition date.

extent.description: At the encoded-unit level, 126 counted items comprise 27 prose paragraphs and 99 verse groups, organized here as nine medieval text groups plus a noncontiguous editorial-commentary part. The XML has 1,001 L elements: 804 Middle English and 197 Latin. Five Latin headings embedded as L in the Hours are display text, leaving 996 verse-line elements; four Middle English comparison lines quoted in German commentary remain included because they are verse quotations. Printed page coverage is complete from p. 283 through p. 319.

formStatements.description: Modern German scholarly prose and Middle English/Latin verse.
German prose with four quoted Middle English verse lines in section 3.
The selected medieval texts are verse or verse excerpts.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Five Latin hour or display headings in the Hours are encoded as L elements but are not counted as verse lines.
Dates, places, authorship, and form are scoped to the article or named parts; properties of one witness are not transferred to all components.
The XML header LANG value 'enm ger' omits substantive Latin, so the catalog records Latin from the body itself.
Entity-specific facsimile rights and availability are documented in the lineage manifest and must not be inherited across providers.

next_steps: Request target images or consult a current foliation concordance.
Do not extrapolate a universal one-folio offset from the English poem's historical 64b/current 64v agreement.

next_steps: Request whole-object imaging from the Bodleian, British Library, Caius, Lincoln Cathedral, and Cambridge University Library using the exact shelfmarks and work locators in the lineage manifest.
Record provider terms before redistributing supplied images.

next_steps: Acquire complete witnesses and collate component by component.
Do not infer a single stemma for this heterogeneous article.

notes: This broad cultural origin does not assign a common place of composition to the separate works.
Heuser's dialect assessment is not automatically a place-of-composition claim.
Richard FitzRalph and Richard Kilmington are discussed in connection with antifraternal material, but the consulted sources do not establish either as author of Heuser's Latin defense poem.

scope.description: The distinct medieval texts and excerpts assembled in the article
Selection and arrangement of the article's manuscript transcriptions, re-edited passages, quotations, and commentary
Passages Heuser explicitly says he gives from Rawlinson poet. 175, Cotton Cleopatra B II, Caius 175/96, and Trinity O.9.38

subjects.label: Verse refrains built around the vocables O and I
Passion of Christ and the Cross

summaries.value: Wilhelm Heuser's 1904 study links a newly printed four-Evangelist refrain cycle to the Expanded Northern Homily Cycle, then compares further Middle English and Latin works whose refrains turn on O and I. The article prints complete works, selected homily and antifraternal excerpts, variant-supported re-editions, and an addendum containing an Hours of the Cross and a Latin defense of friars. CME00096 preserves the complete article rather than a single medieval poem.

The XML contains two DIV1 units, six DIV2 sections, thirteen DIV3 units, 27 German prose paragraphs, 99 line groups, and 1,001 L elements across printed pp. 283-319. Of the L elements, 804 are Middle English and 197 Latin; five Latin hour/display headings in the Hours are not verse, yielding 996 verse lines represented. The intellectual parts below preserve Heuser's source and work boundaries rather than treating every XML container as a separate work.

Heuser directly transcribed four groups from manuscripts, printed selected Harley homily passages, reused earlier editions by Wright and Morris, and based the Adam poem chiefly on Horstmann while adding Cambridge variants. Those distinct paths are preserved in the linked lineage graph. The complete Trinity O.9.38 object is publicly imaged; no complete public facsimile was verified for the other direct witnesses.

notes: Heuser's English-language article title is retained for the heterogeneous edited selection; it is not a collective title found in a medieval witness.
